body:
Thanks, @sapla1125. The underlying SSRF concern in #7315 is legitimate. Closing this PR, though, with a redirect on what the actual fix needs to look like.

The biggest reason: the code doesn't do what the title, description, and README all claim. The README update reads "Axios now throws an explicit error if a URL has a protocol but is missing `//`," but the implementation only throws on non-string inputs (`null`, `undefined`, numbers, objects). `parseProtocol('https:google.com')` still returns `'https'` without throwing. The tests confirm it, they cover the type-check cases, but never assert a throw on `http:/example.com` or `https:/127.0.0.1/` despite the description saying they do.

The other thing worth flagging is that `parseProtocol` isn't where the normalisation happens. It's a scheme-extraction helper used to gate the unsupported-protocol check in `xhr.js`. The actual `https:google.com` → `https://google.com/` rewrite is WHATWG `URL` behaviour, which happens inside `new URL(fullPath)` in the http adapter, and inside the browser's XMLHttpRequest URL parser. By the time `parseProtocol` runs, nothing has been normalised yet, and by the time normalisation happens, `parseProtocol` has already returned. Even a corrected version of this PR wouldn't reach the SSRF case.

Closing. If you'd like to take a swing at the validator-before-URL-parse approach above, happy to review the follow-up.

author: jasonsaayman

body:
> @harsh-parakh22 this does make a breaking change from what i can see, can you elabortate on why you think this may be a good change

The motivation here is that for synchronous request interceptors an error thrown by the interceptor currently does not reliably propagate as a rejected promise. Instead, the request flow can continue in ways that mask the original failure which makes interceptor error handling harder to reason about.

The change aligns sync interceptor behavior with the existing async interceptor semantics, if an interceptor throws, the request should reject with that error rather than continuing dispatch. This makes error propagation explicit and consistent, and avoids surprising cases where failures are silently swallowed or replaced downstream.

That said, I agree this is a behavior change and could affect existing expectations

body:
Thanks for the work on this. I dug into it and unfortunately there's a regression I don't think we can land as-is.

The existing implementation wraps every incoming abort reason. Anything that isn't already an `AxiosError` becomes a `CanceledError` (which extends `AxiosError`). That invariant is load-bearing: the fetch adapter at `lib/adapters/fetch.js:415` checks `composedSignal.reason instanceof AxiosError` to recover from Safari's broken DOMException-like aborts (added in #10806).

`AbortSignal.any` propagates the source signal's reason as-is, with no wrapping. So if a user does the most ordinary thing, calling `controller.abort()` with no reason, or `controller.abort(new Error('cancelled'))`, the composed signal's reason is no longer an `AxiosError`. The Safari fast path is skipped and the user gets a generic `Network Error` instead of their cancellation propagating cleanly.

A correct version needs to keep the wrapping. Roughly: use `AbortSignal.any` internally, but route its abort through an outer controller that re-wraps non-`AxiosError` reasons. At that point the gain over the current implementation is pretty small, but still worth doing for clarity.

Going to close this in favor of an issue so it can be picked up with the wrapping preserved and a regression test for the `controller.abort()` case. Really do appreciate the effort, the direction is right, the missing piece is reason normalization.

body: Hey @VikramSingh1994 :) > Are there any plans to fully address this issue and clear all related vulnerabilities in the 0.x branch. The vulnerability was fixed in version `0.30.3` of axios. That's exactly what this PR (and the linked release) was all about. > We’re currently seeing security scanners continue to flag the 0.x version I have never used Snyk myself, but I guess that they are showing outdated vulnerability information then. They probably automatically flagged version `0.30.3` as vulnerable (after its release), because the affected version list still simply says "every version `< 1.13.5` is affected". Note that the NPM registry (for `npm audit`) is fetching data from the **GitHub advisory database**, https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-43fc-jf86-j433 in this case. For NPM to pick up the fix, I had to manually propose the new affected version ranges over there. The affected version ranges should look like this now: ``` >= 1.0.0, <= 1.13.4 <= 0.30.2 ``` The GitHub advisory database allows everyone to propose changes via the "[Suggest improvements for this vulnerability](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-43fc-jf86-j433/improve)" link on each page. Snyk does the same via the "[Found a mistake?](https://support.snyk.io/s/contactsupport)" button. You have to have an account for this though, which I don't have. Feel free to make them aware of the fixed vulnerability in version `0.30.3` by clicking the button and contacting their support. https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-AXIOS-15252993

body:
Isn't this a retry of #7257? 
SilentJSONParsing has no effect without explicitly specifying JSON as the expected response type, because historically, the default responseType in axios is **"auto", not "json"**. This is simply an inaccuracy in the documentation. 
SilentJSONParsing only controls silent parsing for the JSON response type since we clearly expect JSON. This isn't a bug, but a design specificity, so changing it will lead to a breaking change, which is unacceptable within a single major version. 
All transitional options are temporary within a single major version. This is a way to change a feature's behavior in a single major version without introducing breaking changes to follow SEMVER guidelines. Otherwise, we would have to frequently release major versions to implement some functionality that introduces breaking changes.

## KA-CHOW Automated Review

**Change Type:** REFACTOR
**Risk Level:** LOW

### Summary
This PR refactors the AxiosError toJSON method to exclude cancelToken from config, affecting the core service and its dependents. The change is a refactor and does not alter the API contract or schema. The refactored method is intended to improve the library's functionality without introducing breaking changes.

### Affected Areas
* core
* .github
* bin
* examples
* adapters
* cancel

### Why This Matters
The refactor may have a downstream impact on services that rely on the AxiosError toJSON method, but the low risk level suggests that the impact should be minimal. 

### Recommended Action
Review the changes and test the affected services to ensure the refactor did not introduce any unintended behavior, verifying that the library's functionality remains intact.

body:
Hi @digital-wizard48 

I think this is a bit of an ambitious upgrade, the packages in axios are mainly as old as they are to enable really deep long term support for outdated versions of node and browsers. I am working on a plan to make it possible to have more modern packages and tooling but maintain backward compatibility as far as possible. Due to this for now im going to close this PR as it would be a very large ask for me to expect you to get this working with the older versions.

Thanks for your intent to contribute to open source!

body:
Hi, The new Node http adapter test requires `AbortController.abort(reason)` / `AbortSignal.reason` support, but CI runs Node 14 where abort reasons are not supported; the assertion on `err.message === abortReason` will fail and break CI.

**Severity:** action required | **Category:** reliability

**How to fix:** Gate test by runtime support

**Agent prompt to fix** - you can give this to your LLM of choice:

> ### Issue description
> The new unit test assumes `AbortController.abort(reason)` and `AbortSignal.reason` exist. CI runs Node 14, where abort reasons are not supported, so the test will fail.
> 
> ### Issue Context
> CI matrix includes Node 14.x.
> 
> ### Fix Focus Areas
> - test/unit/adapters/http.js[2469-2488]
> - .github/workflows/run-ci.yml[19-22]
> 
> ### Suggested fix
> In the test, feature-detect support (e.g., `if (!('reason' in AbortController.prototype.signal ?? controller.signal))` or check `typeof controller.abort === 'function'` plus `('reason' in controller.signal)` after aborting with a reason) and skip/adjust assertion for Node 14. Alternatively, split assertions: always assert cancellation, and only assert message propagation when `signal.reason` is supported.

We noticed a couple of other issues in this PR as well - happy to share if helpful.

---
*[Qodo](https://github.com/marketplace/qodo-merge-pro-for-open-source) code review - free for open-source.*

body:
Hi, The new unit tests perform real HTTP requests to http://localhost:1 for the non-rejection cases, so they depend on environment networking/port behavior and can hang until the mocha timeout. This makes the unit suite slower and potentially flaky across CI environments.

**Severity:** action required | **Category:** reliability

**How to fix:** Mock adapter; avoid real I/O

**Agent prompt to fix** - you can give this to your LLM of choice:

> ### Issue description
> Unit tests in `test/unit/core/dispatchRequest.js` make real network requests to `http://localhost:1` for cases that are meant to verify validation *does not* throw. This introduces flakiness and unnecessary delays.
> 
> ### Issue Context
> These tests only need to exercise `dispatchRequest`’s timeout validation behavior, not real HTTP connectivity.
> 
> ### Fix Focus Areas
> - test/unit/core/dispatchRequest.js[53-113]
> 
> ### Suggested fix
> - Replace the “allow …” tests’ real `axios.get('http://localhost:1', …)` calls with a stubbed adapter (e.g., create an axios instance with `adapter: () => Promise.resolve({ data: '', status: 200, statusText: 'OK', headers: {}, config })`) and assert the request resolves.
> - Alternatively, call `dispatchRequest` directly with a stub adapter, or use an existing local test server helper instead of an arbitrary port.

---
*Spotted by [Qodo](https://github.com/marketplace/qodo-merge-pro-for-open-source) code review - free for open-source projects.*

author: aviu16

body:
Maintainer note on scope vs prior attempt:

- This patch fixes the clamp at the shared progress reducer layer (`lib/helpers/progressEventReducer.js`), not only one adapter hook.
- That means the clamp behavior is centralized for computable progress events across adapter call sites that use this reducer.
- It also guards byte deltas against out-of-order events (`Math.max(0, loaded - bytesNotified)`), which avoids negative `bytes` in callback payloads.
- Added regression test coverage in `test/unit/helpers/progressEventReducer.js` for both overflow (`loaded > total`) and out-of-order event sequences.

If maintainers prefer, I can also add a narrow adapter-level assertion on top of the reducer test, but I kept this patch focused to minimize risk.
author: aviu16

body: Addressed: I tightened the test assertion (expected bytes delta is now exact, not permissive) and updated reducer bookkeeping so bytesNotified remains monotonic across out-of-order events. This keeps clamped progress correct and avoids inflated byte deltas after loaded decreases.

Closing this PR — after reviewing the cubic-dev feedback, I realized the `<picture>` tags with `prefers-color-scheme: dark` media queries are intentional. They serve dark mode logo variants for Descope and Route4Me sponsors. Removing them would cause those logos to render incorrectly on dark backgrounds.

Apologies for the noise.

author: dependabot

body:
Thank you @cubic-dev-ai for the review!

This is a valid concern. The current implementation intentionally includes the full URL in error messages to help developers debug request failures more easily. However, I understand the privacy and security implications.

I'd like to suggest a middle ground approach:
1. Keep the URL in error messages for development/debugging
2. Add an option to redact sensitive query parameters (like , , , ) when errors are logged in production

Would you be open to this approach? Or would you prefer we completely remove the URL from error messages?

Alternatively, we could mask query parameters by default, e.g.,  instead of the full URL.

Let me know your preference and I'll update the implementation accordingly.
author: theluckystrike

body:
To clarify my previous suggestion - I was referring to sensitive query parameters like 'token', 'api_key', 'password', and 'secret'. 

Looking forward to your feedback on the best approach!
author: theluckystrike

body:
> Thank you @cubic-dev-ai for the review!
> 
> This is a valid concern. The current implementation intentionally includes the full URL in error messages to help developers debug request failures more easily. However, I understand the privacy and security implications.
> 
> I'd like to suggest a middle ground approach:
> ...

@theluckystrike I suggested masking or stripping the query parameters because `AxiosError` messages are frequently sent to external logging services or shown in client-side UIs, where sensitive data like `?token=...` or `?api_key=...` can easily leak.

Stripping the query string by default is the safest middle ground. Since the full, original URL remains available programmatically via `error.config.url`, developers can still access it for internal logging if they have a secure sanitization process in place.

In `lib/core/settle.js`, you could modify the URL extraction like this:

```javascript
// Strip query parameters to prevent leaking sensitive data in logs
const url = response.config?.url 
  ? response.config.url.split('?')[0] 
  : 'unknown URL';
```

This ensures the error message identifies the endpoint (e.g., `/api/v1/users`) without exposing the specific parameters that failed. If you want to go a step further, you could add a new configuration property like `redactQueryParams: boolean` to `requestConfig`, but a safe default is usually preferred for a core library update.

https://www.cubic.dev/ai-review?tab=learnings
https://docs.cubic.dev
https://www.cubic.dev/wiki/axios/axios

**Changes made:**
- URL now shows only the path (e.g.,  instead of )
- The full URL with query parameters is still available programmatically via  for secure internal logging

This ensures the error message identifies the endpoint without exposing potentially sensitive query parameters that might leak to external logging services or client-side UIs.

Thanks again for the feedback!

body:
Hi @cubic-dev-ai,

Thank you for the follow-up review! I appreciate the thorough checking.

To address your latest concern about URL sanitization in settle.js, I've already implemented the fix in commit 0c47df6 which strips query parameters from the URL in error messages. This prevents sensitive data (like tokens, api_keys, etc.) from being exposed in error logs while still providing the endpoint information for debugging.

The current implementation:
- Shows only the path (e.g., `/api/v1/users`) in error messages
- Keeps the full URL available programmatically via `error.config.url` for secure internal logging

This follows the best practice of not exposing sensitive query parameters in error surfaces while maintaining debuggability.

Could you please confirm if the current implementation addresses your security concerns? If there's anything else you'd like adjusted, I'm happy to make changes.

Thank you for the detailed reviews!

body:
Addressed the review feedback — `runWhen` in `AxiosInterceptorHandler` is now optional (`?`) to also allow `undefined`, covering all runtime states: `Function`, `null`, and `undefined`.

Changes are backward compatible and fix the TypeScript build failures reported in #7404. This PR is ready to merge.

author: Mridul012

body:
Thanks for the review!

Good catch regarding the merge order and potential serialization risks.

I’ve updated the implementation to spread custom enumerable properties **before** the standard AxiosError fields. This ensures that core fields like `config`, `message`, and others always take precedence, preserving the existing sanitization and preventing circular reference issues.

Additionally, `request`, `response`, and `cause` remain explicitly excluded to avoid serializing non-safe or circular structures.

With this order, we retain custom metadata (e.g., trace IDs) while guaranteeing that no unsafe values override the normalized error shape.

Let me know if you'd like me to add tests specifically covering circular reference edge cases as well.

author: Karthikeya1500

body:
Thanks for picking this up. I spent some time tracing through where this actually lands, and I'm going to close it. Let me explain why.

`forEachEntry` is internal to axios, not exported, and has exactly one caller: `formDataToJSON` at `lib/helpers/formDataToJSON.js:87`. That caller already gates its use:

```js
if (utils.isFormData(formData) && utils.isFunction(formData.entries)) {
  utils.forEachEntry(formData, ...);
}
```

So the only input `forEachEntry` ever sees is an already-validated `FormData`, which always has a conforming iterator. The "potential TypeError" this PR defends against can't actually happen on any real axios codepath.

A few things make this hard to land as-is:

1. The change replaces a clear `TypeError` with a silent return. If something does call this incorrectly in the future, the bug surfaces later as quietly missing data instead of a stack trace pointing at the cause. For a private helper, the loud failure is more useful.

2. The defenses are partial. The PR validates `_iterator.next` is a function, but doesn't validate what `next()` returns. A non-conforming iterator that returns `{}` from `.next()` will infinite-loop because `result.done` is `undefined`. So the "fully hardened" framing isn't quite there, and chasing complete coverage is rabbit-hole territory for an internal helper.

3. The PR description and follow-up comment mention unit tests being added, but the diff only contains `lib/utils.js`. The tests don't appear to have been committed.

If we ever do want to harden this surface, the right place is the public boundary (`formDataToJSON` itself, where someone could in theory pass an unexpected shape), not the internal walker. And it should start from a real reproduction, not a hypothetical.

Going to close. Really do appreciate the contribution, please don't let this put you off. There's plenty in axios that needs attention, happy to point you toward something more concrete if you want to take another swing.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
> HTTP/2 is not supported in browser-like environments

Actually, HTTP2 is supported by browsers' fetch, but it's done automatically under the hood, without the ability to select the protocol version manually. As we can see, in the opinion of the fetch spec designers, adding an option to select exactly the protocol version wasn't their priority, so I think the protocol version isn't critical enough for adding a warning to the frontend code, where code size should be reduced at almost any cost. It would be better to highlight this behaviour in the docs rather than adding a notice to the runtime.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body: Thanks for the contribution. Since opening this, we've taken a docs-only approach to clarifying httpVersion semantics across adapters (#10644, merged), and I've closed the source issue (#7535) on that basis. @DigitalBrainJS also raised a valid concern about adding runtime warnings and bundle size to browser code paths, which I agree with. Closing this in favour of the docs path. If you'd like to follow up, a good next step would be a contribution to expand the HTTP/2 section of the README with concrete adapter-by-adapter behaviour matrices.

body:
Hi, Including `response.data` and `response.headers` in AxiosError.toJSON() can cause sensitive information (PII, tokens, Set-Cookie) to be logged by structured loggers that serialize errors automatically. This is a security behavior change compared to omitting `response` entirely.

**Severity:** remediation recommended | **Category:** security

**How to fix:** Redact sensitive headers/data

**Agent prompt to fix** - you can give this to your LLM of choice:

> ### Issue description
> `AxiosError.toJSON()` now serializes `response.data` and `response.headers`, which may contain secrets (e.g., `set-cookie`) or PII and could be logged unintentionally.
> 
> ### Issue Context
> This method is commonly used for logging/serialization; adding response contents changes what gets emitted.
> 
> ### Fix Focus Areas
> - lib/core/AxiosError.js[69-77]
> 
> ### Suggested mitigations (choose one)
> - Redact known-sensitive response headers (e.g., `set-cookie`) from the serialized headers object.
> - Consider omitting `response.data` by default or only including it when it is a plain JSON value (and/or small), to reduce accidental leakage.
> - Document this behavior clearly if it is intended.

We noticed a couple of other issues in this PR as well - happy to share if helpful.

---
*Found by [Qodo](https://qodo.ai) code review*

body:
## Review fixes pushed — all 14 findings addressed

Thanks cubic-dev-ai for the thorough review. All findings have been fixed in commit \`7bb37ec\`.

### P1 fixes (3 critical):

| Finding | Fix |
|---------|-----|
| **Path traversal** in \`readScriptFile\` | Resolved path is now validated to stay within \`pkgDir\` — \`../\` traversal silently rejected |
| **Nested node_modules** not scanned | \`enumeratePackages\` now recurses into nested \`node_modules/\` with symlink loop protection |
| **Permissive allowlist** matching | \`contentHash\` is now **required** when a script file exists — missing hash = potential tampering, flagged as HIGH |

### P2 fixes (8 moderate):

| Finding | Fix |
|---------|-----|
| **Comment bypass** in phantom detection | New \`stripCommentsAndStrings()\` preprocessor removes comments before scanning |
| **Shell injection** via version string | Semver regex validation before \`execSync\` interpolation |
| **Unsigned baseline** accepted in CI | Now FAILS in CI mode when \`DEPS_BASELINE_SECRET\` is missing |
| **Transitive deps** only checked hoisted | Now checks both hoisted (top-level) and nested locations |
| **drift \|\| true** suppresses failures | Removed — drift scan failures now properly fail the job |
| **publish-gate** never triggers | Added \`tags: v*.*.*\` to workflow triggers |
| **npm test \|\| echo** masks failures | Removed fallback — test failures now block publish |
| **npm view** failure masked | Registry errors now FAIL post-publish verification |
| **Safe version** guidance ambiguous | Explicitly excludes compromised versions (1.14.1, 0.30.4) |

Every fix maintains the zero-dependency constraint. Scripts re-tested locally.
author: Valisthea

body:
## Round 2 fixes pushed — 3 remaining findings addressed

Commit \`8b35a5b\` fixes all 3 findings from the second review pass:

| Finding | Fix |
|---------|-----|
| **Symlink bypass** in path traversal guard | Both paths now canonicalized via \`fs.realpathSync\` before comparison — symlinks inside pkgDir pointing outside are caught |
| **Baseline key collision** for nested packages | State keys now use relative dir path (\`node_modules/a/node_modules/b:postinstall\`) instead of just \`b:postinstall\` — no more overwrites between different instances |
| **String literal bypass** in phantom detection | \`stripCommentsAndStrings\` now actually strips string literals on non-import lines — \`const s = "require('evil-pkg')"\` no longer counts as a real import |

All three fixes maintain zero external dependencies. Scripts re-tested locally.

body:
Hello, after reading #10633, I found out about zizmor and this PR.
I was wondering if zizmor is protected from being deleted by an hacker (with a commit from the hacked dev)?
And how is it stopping `publish.yml` workflow to be called (with a git tag from the hacked dev)?
author: daiyam

body:
@daiyam 

Based off https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/10636#issuecomment-4189893462, it looks like you've already been tinkering with zizmor, so maybe you've answered your own questions by now :-). But in case you haven't:

zizmor isn't a runtime security gate; it's a static analysis linter for GitHub Actions workflow (and related) files, similar to what ESLint is for JavaScript. It scans your workflow YAML for insecure patterns during development (e.g.,unpinned action references, injection-prone expressions, etc.). It runs in CI as a check on PRs so reviwers can catch workflow security issues before they're merged. [(zizmor-action](https://github.com/zizmorcore/zizmor-action), which is used here, also uploads results as SARIF to GitHub's Security tab (Code Scanning alerts), so findings are tracked alongside other security alerts for the repo.)

It doesn't stop a compromised dev from triggering a workflow or pushing a tag; that's not it's job. Gating workflow execution is the job of e.g., branch protection rules and GitHub Environments (required reviewers, branch restrictions, etc.). 

(One thing to note if anyone _does_ try to remove the zizmor workflow, it will have to be done publicly via git, so at the very least there's an audit trail.)

And as discussed in #10636, none of these CI-level controls would have prevented this specific attack anyway, since the attacker published directly   to the npm registry from the compromised machine without going through CI at all.
author: shaanmajid

body: @shaanmajid Thank you for your reply.

body:
> @cubic-dev-ai Could you please read this comment?
> The current implementation does not prebuffer FormData. Content-Length is only computed for body types that can be measured without consuming the body: Blob (via .size), ArrayBuffer/views (via .byteLength), strings, and URLSearchParams. FormData and streams are explicitly skipped — no buffering occurs for those types. The comment in the code documents this: // FormData and streams cannot be re-read.
> Thanks.
> 
> 

@Kilros0817 You're right, and I appreciate the clarification. Looking closely at the implementation in `lib/adapters/fetch.js`, I see that you've explicitly restricted the `Content-Length` calculation to types with a known size or that can be safely encoded:

- **Blob**: Uses `data.size`.
- **ArrayBuffer/View**: Uses `data.byteLength`.
- **Strings/URLSearchParams**: Uses `encodeText`.

The logic correctly omits `FormData` and streams, which avoids the risk of prematurely consuming the body or causing performance hits from pre-buffering. This approach safely aligns the `fetch` adapter's behavior with the Node `http` adapter for measurable payloads while respecting the limitations of streamable data.

For more details on how these rules are applied, you can check the [Axios wiki](https://www.cubic.dev/wiki/axios/axios).

author: ashstrc

body:
> It also clarifies that options like httpVersion are not recognized and are ignored, which may otherwise lead to confusion.

Hmm. Just curious, have you considered the possibility that `httpVersion` & `http2Options`, and the entire HTTP2 section in the docs, including code example, were added intentionally because they are actually supported, and not as a prank on our users? 😁
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
Good point — thanks for raising this.

From what I observed, `httpVersion` and `http2Options` appear in examples and docs, but their support seems limited to specific adapters or experimental cases, rather than being consistently supported across Axios.

My intention with this PR was to reduce confusion where users might expect full HTTP/2 support just by passing `httpVersion`, which may not work as expected in many environments.

That said, I agree the current docs are a bit ambiguous. Would it make more sense to clarify that HTTP/2 support is experimental / adapter-specific rather than stating it's ignored entirely?
author: ashstrc

body:
> Would it make more sense to clarify that HTTP/2 support is experimental / adapter-specific 

This is exactly what is written there:

> In version 1.13.0, **experimental** HTTP2 support was added to the **http** adapter

I think it's pretty obvious that support depends on the environment, and if a Node.js version or other server environment doesn't have a compatible API for HTTP2, then Axios won't support it. Although you could add a remark for this in the documentation. 
P.S Axios is not officially tested for compatibility with Bun or Deno.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
Thanks for the clarification — that makes sense.

I see now that HTTP/2 support exists but is adapter-specific and environment-dependent.

My intention was mainly to highlight that options like `httpVersion` may not behave consistently across environments, which can be confusing for users.

I’ll update the PR to clarify these limitations instead of stating that HTTP/2 is unsupported.
author: ashstrc

body:
Good catch — thanks for pointing that out.

I've removed the incorrect statement and updated the section to reflect that HTTP/2 support is experimental and adapter-dependent rather than unsupported.

Also restored the emoji in the section heading as requested.

author: socket-security

body:
Thanks for the patch. I'm going to close this one. The underlying issue is real, but smaller than the description suggests, and the diff has some problems that I'd want fixed before reworking the approach.

I tried reproducing #6801 against the current v1.x HEAD with two cases: a stalling TCP server (accepts the connection, never writes) and an unreachable host (192.0.2.1, TEST-NET-1). In both cases, the request always settled. So "the promise never settles" doesn't match what v1.x is doing today.

There is still a real bug, just narrower: with `maxRedirects: 0`, configured timeouts shorter than the kernel's TCP SYN-retry window (~5s on macOS) get swallowed during the connect phase. The reject only fires once the kernel gives up. The `maxRedirects > 0` path doesn't have this problem because follow-redirects starts its own timer at request construction. I'll open a separate issue scoped to that.

On the diff itself, a few things would need to change:

1. The `new AxiosError(...)` call has an extra positional argument, which shifts everything by one. The `'ETIMEDOUT'` string ends up in the `config` slot, the actual config goes into `request`, and `req` ends up in `response`. That corrupts the error shape for anyone catching it.
2. `socket.setTimeout(timeout)` is added with no `'timeout'` listener, so nothing reacts when it fires. `req.setTimeout(ms, cb)` already handles the post-connect case.
3. The `if (req.destroyed && isDone) return` guard never short-circuits when it'd matter — `isDone` only flips after settle, and the timeout fires before that.
4. No tests for the new behaviour, and the description claims the code standardises to `ETIMEDOUT` while the code still uses the `clarifyTimeoutError` ternary.

Closing in favour of a focused fix tracked in the new issue.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
According to https://github.com/axios/axios#semver, Axios follows semver.

My understanding of this change is that the `setHeader()` function started throwing an error now, which it didn't before, and that this is generally considered a breaking change.

I noticed this in https://github.com/mdn/mdn-http-observatory/pull/489, because our tests failed. For our use case in the MDN HTTP Observatory, we would prefer if `setHeader()` didn't throw, or if there was an opt-out mechanism.
author: caugner

body: Thanks, @caugner. I should have checked more cases, as I had determined this to be quite low risk for 99% of users. I will add an explicit opt-out and make a PR to the MDN lib as soon as it is ready.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Thanks. I'm wondering how other HTTP request frameworks handle invalid headers. Do they throw, or do they ignore or sanitize the value instead?
author: caugner

body: I don't actually know. I will do some research. Would it be fine in your case if this is just sanitised and does not throw? That would be a better approach for us. In the past, security opt-in has led to the same CVEs being opened repeatedly.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
Would it be an option to have two modes for handling `\r` and `\n` in headers:

- loose (default) replaces them with space, and
- strict (opt-in) throws an error.

You could then make strict the default option in Axios v2.
author: caugner

body: Sure, that can work! Thanks for the suggestion. I will do that asap.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
[RFC 9110 defines](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#name-field-values) the grammar of HTTP field values as follows:

```abnf
  field-value    = *field-content
  field-content  = field-vchar
                   [ 1*( SP / HTAB / field-vchar ) field-vchar ]
  field-vchar    = VCHAR / obs-text
  obs-text       = %x80-FF
```

(VCHAR is [defined in RFC 5234](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5234#:~:text=%25x21%2D7E))

So strictly speaking the `isValidHeaderValue()` function is not complete, and could be something like this:

```js
const isValidHeaderValue = (value) =>
  // HTAB:    \x09 (not allowed as first/last character)
  // SP:      \x20 (not allowed as first/last character)
  // VCHAR:   \x20-\x7E
  // obs-text \x80-\xFF
  /^[\x09\x20-\x7E\x80-\xFF]+$/.test(value) && !/^[\x09\x20]|[\x09\x20]$/.test(value);
```

Edit: Loose mode could strip forbidden characters.

```js
const sanitizeHeaderValue = (value) =>
  value
    .replace(/[^\x09\x20-\x7E\x80-\xFF]/g, '')
    .replace(/^[\x09\x20]|[\x09\x20]$/g, '');
```

body:
@cubic-dev-ai The stale closure issue has been addressed. 
The fix now stores the active request on the socket itself via 
`socket[SOCKET_CURRENT_REQUEST]`, which is updated on every 
`req.on('socket', ...)` callback. The error handler reads this 
reference at error-time rather than closing over the original `req`, 
ensuring correct rejection on keep-alive reused connections.
author: vitingr

body:
> @cubic-dev-ai The stale closure issue has been addressed. 
> The fix now stores the active request on the socket itself via 
> `socket[SOCKET_CURRENT_REQUEST]`, which is updated on every 
> `req.on('socket', ...)` callback. The error handler reads this 
> reference at error-time rather than closing over the original `req`, 
> ...

@vitingr This updated approach effectively solves the stale closure problem. By moving the request reference to a property on the socket (`socket[SOCKET_CURRENT_REQUEST]`) and updating it every time the `socket` event fires, you've ensured that the singleton error listener always acts on the active request context.

Using Symbols is the right choice here to avoid name collisions on the socket object, which is especially important since these sockets are often managed by a global or shared `http.Agent`. This implementation safely handles the lifecycle of keep-alive connections where the same socket instance is reused across multiple Axios calls.

One detail to note: since `SOCKET_CURRENT_REQUEST` is only deleted on `close`, an error occurring on an idle socket sitting in the connection pool will still trigger `currentReq.destroy(err)` on the *last* request that used it. This is perfectly acceptable as it still prevents the process crash (the primary goal) and calling `destroy` on a completed request is a no-op in Node.js.

You can find more about how I track these types of patterns in [my documentation](https://docs.cubic.dev) or view your team's saved [repository learnings](https://www.cubic.dev/ai-review?tab=learnings).

author: jasonsaayman

body:
> Thanks for picking this up. Please check the feedback:
> 
> ## 1. `err.status` is left undefined (correctness bug)
> `AxiosError`'s constructor normalises `status` from `response`:
> 
> ```js
> if (response) {
>   this.response = response;
>   this.status = response.status;
> }
> (lib/core/AxiosError.js:49-52)
> ```
> 
> Both patched paths construct the error without response, then assign `err.response = response` afterwards. That bypasses the normalisation, so `err.status` stays `undefined` even though `err.response.status` is populated. `AxiosError.toJSON()` serialises the status directly, so logged/serialised errors will be inconsistent.
> 
> The convention already used elsewhere in this same file (see line 930) is to pass the response as the 5th argument:
> 
> ```js
> responseStream.on('aborted', function handlerStreamAborted() {
>   if (rejected) return;
>   const err = new AxiosError(
>     'stream has been aborted',
>     AxiosError.ERR_BAD_RESPONSE,
>     config,
>     lastRequest,
>     response
>   );
>   responseStream.destroy(err);
>   reject(err);
> });
> 
> responseStream.on('error', function handleStreamError(err) {
>   if (req.destroyed) return;
>   reject(AxiosError.from(err, null, config, lastRequest, response));
> });
> ```
> 
> This removes the post-hoc assignment, sets status correctly, and drops the now-unnecessary if (response) guard (which is unreachable anyway, response is const-declared at line 839 in the same closure, before the listeners are attached).
> 
> ## 2. Please add a regression test
> This closes a long-standing issue (#6935), and there are no tests. At a minimum, one test per path:
> 
> * aborted: server writes headers, then `res.destroy()` mid-body → assert `err.response.status`, `err.response.headers`, and `err.status`.
> * error: same setup with an emitted stream error → same assertions.
> 
> Asserting `err.status` in particular would have caught issue #1.
> 
> ## 3. Scope clarification (optional, for the PR description)
> This fix only applies to buffered responses. When responseType: 'stream', the adapter takes the early branch at line 847, and these listeners are never attached. Worth calling out so users with streamed responses don't expect the same behaviour. Also worth noting that err.response.data will be undefined on the aborted path since data is only assigned in the 'end' handler; only metadata (status/headers) survives, which matches the stated intent.
> 
> Once 1 and 2 are addressed, I think this is good to go. Patch-level change, no breaking concerns.

Thanks, addressed in 47d5c57.

Changes made:
- Passed `response` into `AxiosError` / `AxiosError.from(...)` directly for both buffered response stream `aborted` and `error` paths, so `err.status` is normalized correctly.
- Added regression coverage for both paths asserting `err.response.status`, `err.response.headers`, and `err.status`.

Validated locally:
- `npm run test:vitest:unit -- tests/unit/adapters/http.test.js`
- `npx eslint lib/adapters/http.js`
- `git diff --check`

Scope note: this remains limited to buffered responses; `responseType: 'stream'` still takes the early stream branch. On the aborted path, only response metadata is available, so `err.response.data` remains unset.

body:
Thanks for digging into this. You're right, `transformRequest` and `transformResponse` are left-fold pipelines, so data: D is only honest for the first function in the chain. Once any transformer returns a serialised form (`string`, `Buffer`, `FormData`, whatever), the next one isn't looking at D anymore.
  
I don't think that invalidates the PR, though. It just means the generic has a narrower scope than a fully sound pipeline type would.

I added a JSDoc on both interfaces. It explains the pipeline semantics, calls out that D/T only describes the first call, warns that later stages may see a serialised form, and says when typing D is safe and when you need a runtime check.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
> I don't think that invalidates the PR, though. It just means the generic has a narrower scope than a fully sound pipeline type would.

but this is precisely what makes this PR invalid!

> It explains the pipeline semantics, calls out that D/T only describes the first call

> I added a JSDoc on both interfaces. It explains the pipeline semantics, calls out that D/T only describes the first call, warns that later stages may see a serialised form

But the interface and the code itself don't distinguish between the first call and subsequent calls.
This feels like **workaround**...
This should be in the typescript constraints, not in the documentation.

then it makes sense to simply not provide the API in order to create more than 1 transform request (but then backward compatibility will be lost)...

> and says when typing D is safe and when you need a runtime check.

**It turns out that the typings needed for compile-time do not describe what happens at runtime.**
then the whole meaning is lost from the using of typescript....
